original_abstract: Data validation is the critical process of determining whether a dataset is suitable for its intended use. By formalizing the requirements that govern this decision, we can achieve unambiguous communication, enable the automation of assessment workflows, and create a framework for investigating the internal logic of the validation process itself. This article provides a formal definition of data validation and explores the theoretical properties that emerge from it. Specifically, we demonstrate how a rigorous mathematical view allows for the systematic classification of data quality requirements, ordering them by increasing levels of structural complexity. We further explore the subtle logical challenges—such as redundancies and contradictions—that arise when combining multiple validation rules into a single assessment framework.
humanized_abstract: Data validation is the process of determining whether a dataset is suitable for use. By formalizing the constraints that determine valid data we ensure that we communicate clearly and unambiguously, automate tedious workflows and finally that we gain insight into the structure of the validation process itself. In this blog post we give a formal representation of data validation and investigate its properties in depth. We give a formal definition of the validation process along with a mathematical representation of the properties it fulfills. Based on this representation we identify three data quality constraints and show how they can be used to classify data quality constraints in an increasing order of structural complexity. We investigate the logical subtleties behind combining validation rules into a validation framework. Including data validation redundancies and inconsistencies.
